title: A Woman First

jokes.text: Suddenly now white lives matter?
jokes.setup: Someone says 'I had to see you' urgently, implying sudden concern
jokes.punchline: The retort weaponizes the BLM slogan to mock the person's selective concern
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical political joke that repurposes a culturally loaded slogan as a cutting retort. Works as quick wit but lacks character specificity — we don't know the speaker so it can't be fully appreciated.

jokes.text: What is the host of Christmas past doing here?
jokes.setup: A TV host named Jane arrives unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: Riffs on 'Ghost of Christmas Past' to mock Jane's age/longevity in broadcasting
jokes.explanation: A clever, economical age joke built on a familiar cultural reference. The 'host/ghost' substitution is sharp without being showy. Gets real mileage from a single word swap.

jokes.text: Oh, good. 'Cause all this learning is giving me a yamaka-ache.
jokes.setup: Someone says conversion to Judaism is about commitment to the Jewish lifestyle
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing 'yarmulke' as 'yamaka' while making a headache pun — double incompetence joke
jokes.explanation: A layered joke: the mispronunciation signals the character's shallowness about the conversion, and it's also a groan-worthy pun. The double stupidity (wrong word, bad pun) reads as intentional character-embedded comedy. Very quotable and efficient.

jokes.text: Just the two of us. — And Gary. — So passionate.
jokes.setup: Selina describes a romantic vacation — 'just the two of us'
jokes.punchline: Gary, her bag man/personal attendant, was apparently also there, which someone notes, and Selina continues without acknowledgment: 'So passionate.'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured three-beat joke that tells us everything about Gary's relationship with Selina — he's so absorbed into her life that a romantic vacation isn't complete without him, and she doesn't even register the absurdity. The deadpan continuation ('So passionate') is the perfect punchline. Deeply character-integrated.

jokes.text: Oh, and not to mention the topless beach. — I didn't see anything.
jokes.setup: Selina mentions the topless beach on the romantic vacation
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately and defensively claims he saw nothing — implying he was absolutely watching
jokes.explanation: A fast, perfectly timed character beat. Gary's immediate protest is the punchline — nobody asked, which makes it funnier. Classic Veep economy.

jokes.text: Catherine's been diagnosed with an incompetent cervix. — Well, why should her cervix be any different than the rest of her?
jokes.setup: Marjorie gravely delivers the medical diagnosis of an incompetent cervix
jokes.punchline: Selina's instant, cruel redirect to Catherine's general incompetence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. It requires zero setup beyond the medical term. The punchline is instantaneous and perfectly Selina — cutting her own daughter at the worst possible moment with complete casualness. The joke is so mean and so perfectly in character that it reaches the upper tier. Comparable in caliber to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Before you even ask, we have taken a break from sex, specifically penetrative sex. — She has a penis? — I don't know.
jokes.setup: Catherine pre-emptively announces they've stopped penetrative sex due to her cervical condition
jokes.punchline: Selina's confusion about whether Jaffar has a penis; Gary's equally confused 'I don't know'
jokes.explanation: A rapid two-beat escalation. Selina's genuine confusion is funny, but Gary echoing 'I don't know' is the kicker — he's somehow equally in the dark. The joke works on Selina's obliviousness to her own son-in-law and the absurdity of Gary not knowing either.

jokes.text: Well, this isn't bed rest, this is couch rest.
jokes.setup: Catherine is on the couch, supposedly on doctor-ordered bed rest
jokes.punchline: Selina's pedantic correction misses the medical gravity entirely
jokes.explanation: A small but well-crafted character beat. Selina's concern for semantics over her daughter's health is perfectly on-brand. Economy is excellent — one line does the work.

jokes.text: Did you put a towel or anything underneath her? Because, girls, this is imported French linen. — Maybe a waterproof tarp. — Yes, actually, or even a garbage bag, you know, in case she leaks.
jokes.setup: Selina's pregnant, medically at-risk daughter is lying on the couch
jokes.punchline: Selina's primary concern is the upholstery; Gary escalates to a garbage bag in case Catherine 'leaks'
jokes.explanation: A sustained cringe escalation that builds well. Selina's narcissism reaches a new low (French linen over daughter's medical crisis), and Gary's 'garbage bag' topper is perfect — he's so conditioned to please Selina that he enthusiastically endorses dehumanizing Catherine.

jokes.text: "First Woman." — Yeah. — You could get murdered in my country for saying something like that. — It's a bit of a pun. — No, it's not.
jokes.setup: The book title 'First Woman' is revealed; Jaffar notes you could be murdered for that in his country
jokes.punchline: The attempted defense that it's a pun is flatly rejected
jokes.explanation: A two-part joke: Jaffar's casual aside about murder in his country is dark and funny, and then the deflating 'No, it's not' on the pun defense adds a second beat. Sets up a running gag about whether the title is a pun.

jokes.text: There's a typo on the first page. — The first sentence. — 'From the moment I entered the the White House.'
jokes.setup: Brand new advance copies of the book have just arrived to celebration
jokes.punchline: The very first sentence has a doubled 'the' — a copy editing failure on the most prominent text
jokes.explanation: A solid cringe joke — the embarrassment is immediate. The escalation to blaming Amy is good, but the typo itself is a fairly standard 'things go wrong at the worst moment' beat.

jokes.text: Well, that's on you, Amy, because you were in charge of the book. — That's copy editing. — Congratulations again, Amy.
jokes.setup: Selina blames Amy for the typo; Amy correctly notes it's copy editing
jokes.punchline: Richard congratulates Amy anyway — an oblivious non sequitur that ignores the entire conflict
jokes.explanation: Richard's oblivious 'Congratulations again, Amy' is a perfect character beat — he processes none of the conflict and cheerfully congratulates her in the middle of her being blamed. Very Richard, very funny.

jokes.text: Now that's a pun!
jokes.setup: Earlier debate about whether 'First Woman' is a pun
jokes.punchline: Jaffar declares something else is 'now THAT's a pun' — implying the book title still isn't one
jokes.explanation: A quick callback to the running pun dispute. Works well as a throwaway that rewards attention.

jokes.text: Which, again, is tomorrow night, not tonight, despite the title.
jokes.setup: Richard confirms the Tonight Show booking for 'tomorrow night'
jokes.punchline: He can't resist noting the logical confusion inherent in 'The Tonight Show' airing tomorrow night
jokes.explanation: A Richard Splett special — noticing the obvious logical paradox in 'Tonight Show' airing tomorrow. The joke itself is old, but Richard's compulsion to clarify it is a solid character beat. Functional.

jokes.text: And you're doing the 'Today' show one week from yesterday.
jokes.setup: Staffer explaining the TV schedule; the 'Tonight Show' is 'tomorrow night, not tonight despite the title'
jokes.punchline: The 'Today' show is 'one week from yesterday' — a perfectly absurd bureaucratic way of saying 'six days from now'
jokes.explanation: A nice absurdist bureaucratic joke — the elaborate date math for something that could simply be '6 days from now' captures the studied obfuscation of political staffers. The double joke about Tonight Show / Today Show name confusion is a bonus.

jokes.text: Oh, it's Catherine's uterine tea. — Yeah, it's a broth of red raspberry leaf, black haw, and false unicorn root. — I hope that's going in your mouth.
jokes.setup: Gary describes a legitimately medical-sounding herbal tea
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I hope that's going in your mouth' implies the alternative is gynecological — a perfectly timed crass double entendre
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed innuendo. The list of odd ingredients sets up the 'other use' implication, and Selina's delivery is calibrated perfectly — she says just enough and no more.

jokes.text: Whatever happened to half a bottle of red wine and three Virginia Slims?
jokes.setup: Gary has just described elaborate uterine tea remedies for Catherine's pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Selina's nostalgic alternative pregnancy 'remedy' is half a bottle of wine and cigarettes
jokes.explanation: A terrific character joke — Selina's casual nostalgia for medically negligent pregnancy practices perfectly encapsulates her narcissism and generational obliviousness. Highly quotable and very Selina.

jokes.text: I'm not mentioned in this book until page 134. — I once dry-shaved that woman's legs under her desk during a cabinet meeting. — Yeah, I'm not in there till 213, and I wrote the book.
jokes.setup: Staff are checking where they appear in the book
jokes.punchline: Gary's complaint reveals the horrifying intimacy of his servitude (dry-shaving legs in cabinet meetings); Amy tops it by noting she wrote the book and barely appears
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. Gary's casual revelation that he dry-shaved Selina's legs under her desk during a cabinet meeting is one of the most horrifying and funny lines of the series — it perfectly encapsulates his complete loss of self. Amy's topper ('I wrote the book') lands with equal impact. Multi-layered, deeply character-integrated, and genuinely shocking.

jokes.text: Ooh, page 93, suckers! 'As Gary poured my tea, I realized the hostages...' Blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.
jokes.setup: Everyone is upset about where they appear in the book
jokes.punchline: Richard triumphantly shows up on page 93 — but his mention is immediately revealed to be a throwaway mid-sentence about hostages
jokes.explanation: A perfect double-beat joke. Richard's gloating 'page 93, suckers!' sets up triumph, immediately undercut by the fact that his mention is a throwaway mid-clause reference on the way to talking about hostages. Classic Richard — wins and loses simultaneously.

jokes.text: Did you make the book? — Just the dedication.
jokes.setup: Others are lamenting their minimal appearances in the book
jokes.punchline: Richard was just in the dedication — which sounds like it could be significant, but given Selina's self-absorption, is probably perfunctory
jokes.explanation: A small, efficient beat. 'Just the dedication' could mean he's most important or least important — the ambiguity and Richard's cheerful delivery make it work.

jokes.text: Oh, God damn it, this office sucks my nard! — A punishment from Congressman Furlong and the speaker and the president. — An astonishing bipartisan agreement.
jokes.setup: Jonah is furious about his terrible office assignment
jokes.punchline: Kent deadpans that all three branches united against Jonah is 'an astonishing bipartisan agreement'
jokes.explanation: Kent's deadpan is pitch-perfect. The joke does political satire work — the rare bipartisan agreement is against Jonah personally — while also being a genuinely clever character burn. Kent's analytical coldness makes the delivery everything.

jokes.text: Let's see if I'm in here. — Jordan, jobs... — Try Ryan. — I know, Kent. I was looking up jobs because they're important to the American people. — R-Y-A... — I know how to spell my last name, Kent.
jokes.setup: Jonah checks the index of Selina's book for his own name
jokes.punchline: Jonah pretends he was looking under 'jobs' for policy reasons, then is offended that Kent starts spelling his name for him
jokes.explanation: A rapid three-beat escalation that reveals Jonah's ego and defensiveness. His pivot to 'jobs' is a terrible save, and Kent's helpfully spelling his name is the perfect condescending response. Very well-constructed for a short exchange.

jokes.text: Are you fucking kidding? I'm not in here. — I ruined her administration, like, four times. — You'd think that'd count for something.
jokes.setup: Jonah discovers he's not in Selina's book
jokes.punchline: His argument for why he should be included is that he ruined her administration four times
jokes.explanation: A superb Jonah moment. Only Jonah would consider sabotaging someone's administration four times as a credential for recognition. The logic is completely wrong yet completely Jonah. Highly quotable, character-perfect, and very funny.

jokes.text: 12 mentions. Adequate.
jokes.setup: Jonah is outraged at having zero mentions; Kent checks his own count
jokes.punchline: Kent finds 12 mentions and pronounces them merely 'adequate' — with complete detachment
jokes.explanation: Two words. 'Adequate.' Kent's complete self-possession and analytical approach to his own historical significance is one of the episode's funniest character moments. The economy is flawless — he needed no more words. Deeply character-integrated.

jokes.text: The shutdown really hurt you in Massachusetts' anus. AKA New Hampshire.
jokes.setup: Kent is briefing Jonah on his poll numbers
jokes.punchline: Kent refers to New Hampshire as 'Massachusetts' anus' as a geographic/political descriptor, then calmly clarifies
jokes.explanation: A standout line. 'Massachusetts' anus' is an original and perfectly specific political geography insult — it has a logic to it that makes it feel earned rather than random. The calm 'AKA New Hampshire' clarification is the perfect Kent delivery. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Whoa, I'm only up five on Skeevy Deevy? — I went to summer camp with that dude and he was too afraid to get changed in front of the other boys. — I thought that was you. — No, I'm pretty sure it was him. — It was you. — How do you know, Kent? Were you at that summer camp?
jokes.setup: Jonah is dismissive of his opponent's modesty at summer camp
jokes.punchline: Kent flatly asserts the embarrassing story was Jonah, Jonah denies it, and then Kent is revealed to have some reason for certainty
jokes.explanation: A well-escalating exchange. The comedy comes from Kent's certainty and Jonah's deflection. The final 'Were you at that summer camp?' is a good button — Jonah can't imagine how Kent knows, but Kent clearly does.

jokes.text: I suppose I should've hired some goons to kick the shit out of you, but you're dating my daughter, so.
jokes.setup: Sherman Tanz comes to withdraw financial support from Jonah
jokes.punchline: His casual consideration of goon violence, moderated only by the incidental detail of the relationship, delivered completely deadpan
jokes.explanation: A perfectly delivered dark comedy line. The casualness of considering goon violence is funny; the 'so' trailing off is perfect — he's not ruling it out, just noting the inconvenience. Very Veep in its matter-of-fact villainy.

jokes.text: If you stay with him, you're cut off. — Okay. — If you change your mind before dinner, we're having Peking Gourmet. — I'm not coming, but order me some slippery shrimp just in case.
jokes.setup: Sherman delivers an ultimatum to his daughter — stay with Jonah and be cut off
jokes.punchline: Shawnee says 'Okay' and accepts being cut off; Sherman then undermines his dramatic exit by ordering slippery shrimp 'just in case'
jokes.explanation: A great two-stage joke. Shawnee's instant acceptance deflates his power, and then his 'order me some slippery shrimp just in case' is a wonderful human moment that undercuts his villainous bluster. The specificity of 'slippery shrimp' is perfect.

jokes.text: Babe, did you give up your inheritance for me? — Are you out of your fucking mind? I still have my trust.
jokes.setup: Jonah is moved that Shawnee chose him over her father's money
jokes.punchline: Shawnee clarifies she still has her trust — she didn't sacrifice anything financial at all
jokes.explanation: A clean setup/punchline. Jonah's romantic reading is immediately deflated by the practical reality. Fast, economical, and character-appropriate for Shawnee's mercenary pragmatism.

jokes.text: As you may have heard, today marks my departure from the 'CBS Morning Show with Dan Egan.' But you'll still be able to find me weekly on our CBS digital platforms. — Podcast. — What?
jokes.setup: Dan is delivering his farewell address on a morning news show, making it sound grand
jokes.punchline: Brie flatly interjects 'Podcast' — cutting through his self-aggrandizement to reveal 'digital platforms' means a podcast
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical. One word — 'Podcast' — does all the work of deflating Dan's grandiose farewell. The indignant 'What?' is a good button. Dan's ego vs. reality is a running theme, and this executes it perfectly.

jokes.text: I mean, you've been with this network for how many decades now? — I mean, Jane... I will always remember Jane covering the moon landing. — I mean, I studied that footage. — That beautiful black and white stuff. — Before me. — It was just fantast... well, regardless...
jokes.setup: Dan is giving an on-air tribute to Jane
jokes.punchline: He keeps accidentally implying Jane is ancient (decades at the network, moon landing, black and white footage), Jane keeps quietly correcting him, and he keeps stumbling forward
jokes.explanation: A sustained cringe sequence. Dan keeps digging deeper with each compliment-insult, and Jane's quiet corrections ('Before me') are perfectly restrained. The escalation structure is excellent — each beat is worse than the last.

jokes.text: I told them you didn't want cake. — Oh, my God, I love this!
jokes.setup: Dan suggests goodbye cake to patch things up with Jane
jokes.punchline: Jane reveals she proactively told them he didn't want cake — a petty, perfect revenge. Dan is delighted by this.
jokes.explanation: A nice character beat — Jane's pettiness is funny, and Dan's delighted reaction to it is funnier. His 'Oh, my God, I love this!' suggests he respects the move even as the target of it.

jokes.text: Is it bad? — Can I have a drink? I feel a little dehydrated. — It's kind of like the wave in 'The Perfect Storm,' except in this case, the wave is made out of shit and our boat is also made out of shit.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if the Washington Post exposé is bad; Amy asks for a drink first
jokes.punchline: Amy's extended metaphor: a wave of shit, on a boat of shit
jokes.explanation: A beautifully crafted metaphor. The double application of 'shit' — both wave and boat — is the key move. It's not just bad news; the vessel itself is also shit. Highly quotable, original metaphor construction.

jokes.text: He had a GED, guys.
jokes.setup: The Post article describes Selina consulting a 'high school drop-out' as an advisor
jokes.punchline: Selina's correction: he had a GED — her defense of the trainer makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Four words and a perfect character beat. Selina's instinct to defend her lover by noting his GED makes her look catastrophically worse. The timing and economy are flawless.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, they're talking about my eye job? — Chinese cover-up over the tweet. — This isn't an article, this is a gang bang on a pinball machine!
jokes.setup: Staffers are reading out increasingly bad revelations from the article
jokes.punchline: Selina's extended sexual/violent metaphor for being attacked from all sides
jokes.explanation: Selina's vivid, chaotic metaphors are a hallmark of the show. 'Gang bang on a pinball machine' is memorably specific — the random trajectory of a pinball works well as an image for being hit from everywhere.

jokes.text: Who despises me like this?! — Well, there's President Montez, Tom James... — The White House maids, the steward. — White working class voters. — Nobody! — Everybody loves you! — Shut up!
jokes.setup: Selina asks who could hate her enough to do this
jokes.punchline: Staffers produce a laundry list including maids, stewards, and white working class voters; Gary insists everybody loves her; Selina shuts it all down
jokes.explanation: An effective group comedy escalation — the list keeps growing in scope and specificity, hitting different demographics with different reasons to hate Selina. Gary's reflexive 'Everybody loves you!' is the perfect counterpoint.

jokes.text: What have you and your 47 tangled chromosomes done?!
jokes.setup: It's revealed Mike's diary is the source of the leak
jokes.punchline: Selina's insult to Mike references a chromosomal abnormality (Down syndrome) as the explanation for his stupidity
jokes.explanation: A dark insult elevated by scientific specificity. '47 tangled chromosomes' feels more crafted and precise than a generic stupid insult. Veep's insults often work by being weirdly clinical in their cruelty.

jokes.text: I should've gotten a diary with a little lock on it, but I didn't wanna lock myself out.
jokes.setup: Mike is apologizing for the diary leak
jokes.punchline: His defense: he didn't get a locked diary because he feared locking himself out of his own diary
jokes.explanation: One of Mike's finest moments. The logic is perfectly circular and deeply stupid — worrying about being locked out of a diary by your own key. It's written as a genuine concern, which is the most Mike thing possible. Character-perfect, highly quotable, and a wonderful piece of absurdist logic.

jokes.text: How long have you known about this? — Since Alabama. — But to be fair, we thought we were on top of it. — Oh, really? Well, now it's on top of you, it's knocked your teeth out, and it's making sweet love to your face!
jokes.setup: Amy and Mike reveal they've known about the diary since Alabama
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating violent/sexual metaphor for having lost control of the situation
jokes.explanation: Another Selina escalating metaphor — this one builds well through three stages (on top of you / knocked your teeth out / making sweet love to your face). The progression from violence to degradation is characteristic Veep.

jokes.text: Get out of here, you great walrus Judas!
jokes.setup: Mike is being dismissed after the diary revelation
jokes.punchline: Selina's insult combines a physical animal comparison with a biblical betrayal reference
jokes.explanation: A classic Veep compound insult. 'Walrus Judas' — the combination of physical buffoonery and ultimate treachery is memorable. Economy of the insult is excellent.

jokes.text: Oh, dang! What's up, white boys? — Yo, you guys ready to drop that rock? — Gellardi, hit me, I'm open.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives at a basketball court where other legislators play
jokes.punchline: Jonah performing exaggerated AAVE to seem relatable on a basketball court — tone-deaf and instantly mortifying
jokes.explanation: Jonah's cringe attempt at basketball court bonhomie is very on-character, though the joke type (white politician performing Blackness) is not especially original. Gets the job done as a cringe setup.

jokes.text: Oh, really? Why are you picking me? — Because you're so tall. — Oh, God, who the fuck invited Dikembe Mutomtard?
jokes.setup: Jonah is picked first in the basketball draft
jokes.punchline: He's picked solely for height; Furlong immediately arrives and delivers a custom-portmanteau insult ('Mutomtard')
jokes.explanation: Furlong's portmanteau insults are a show signature. 'Mutomtard' — Dikembe Mutombo + retard — is specific and cruel in equal measure. The delivery as an entrance line is perfect.

jokes.text: A little word of advice, Congressman, don't wear the shorts. Even Kobe Bryant wouldn't rape you in those things.
jokes.setup: Jonah is on the court in basketball shorts
jokes.punchline: Furlong's 'advice' weaponizes a serious real-world allegation against Kobe Bryant as an insult — implying Jonah is too unattractive even for that
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insults often walk to the dark side, and this one is deliberately transgressive. The shock factor is present but it relies on a real assault allegation as a cheap shortcut. Lower earned_vs_cheap score reflects this. It's very Veep but not the show at its best.

jokes.text: Lookie here, we got old Jesus with a camel toe and his child molester goggles and the headband, of course, 'cause you don't want the sweat getting in your eyes when you're choking them out afterwards.
jokes.setup: Furlong continues cataloguing Jonah's awful basketball outfit
jokes.punchline: Each item of clothing is given an increasingly disturbing crime-adjacent interpretation
jokes.explanation: Furlong's sustained insult cataloguing is a show specialty. The escalation from camel toe to child molester goggles to post-choking prevention is well-structured — each item gets a worse interpretation. Very much a Furlong showcase.

jokes.text: Balls are the only thing keeping me from choking on cocks. Without balls, I'd be swallowing dicks whole just like Joey Chestnut.
jokes.setup: Jonah's aide Will is instructed to explain why they need the ball back
jokes.punchline: Will's improbable sexual/sporting double entendre involving Joey Chestnut, the competitive eater
jokes.explanation: A uniquely bizarre speech from Will — the Joey Chestnut reference is unexpected and makes the whole thing more memorable. The competitive eating / sexual act comparison is original and lands because it's so specifically weird.

jokes.text: Boom goes the dynamite. — You're fired.
jokes.setup: After Will's speech, Jonah celebrates making a basketball shot with 'Boom goes the dynamite'
jokes.punchline: Will is immediately fired
jokes.explanation: A fast button to the Will sequence. 'You're fired' is the most efficient possible punchline and the irony that Will just helped him get the ball back makes the dismissal funnier.

jokes.text: I just got a lecture from a 22-year-old piss twat with a SUNY Binghamton degree who says that she thinks that you chickened out because of the 'Post' article.
jokes.setup: Amy reports back from 'The Tonight Show'
jokes.punchline: Amy's elaborate dismissal of the producer includes her specific degree and the invented 'piss twat' insult
jokes.explanation: Amy's insults have a precision that matches Veep's style. 'SUNY Binghamton degree' is a very specific institutional dig that makes the insult feel earned — it's not random cruelty, it's class-coded snobbishness from someone who works for a Yale grad.

jokes.text: My presidency just got caught with a tranny hooker on Sunset Boulevard, and I have to make America think that I was just giving her a ride home.
jokes.setup: Selina is strategizing about the damage control response
jokes.punchline: Her metaphor for the situation — which works as a commentary on how political spin actually functions
jokes.explanation: Selina's political metaphors work when they reveal something true about politics. This one (spin = claiming you were giving the hooker a ride home) is sharp political satire embedded in the metaphor.

jokes.text: Actually, I did write a zinger for 'The Tonight Show' about Ray. 'In sleeping with my trainer, I think you could say I did not exercise good judgment.'
jokes.setup: In the middle of the crisis, Mike offers a prepared joke
jokes.punchline: The pun is competent but completely tone-deaf given the severity of the moment
jokes.explanation: The joke within the joke: the pun is mediocre (which is the point — it's Mike), but what makes it funny is the timing. Mike offering a light zinger during a career-destroying crisis is perfect character comedy. Also foreshadows the actual Tonight Show moment.

jokes.text: And maybe give him some of that lesbian cooze potion.
jokes.setup: Making Leon West wait; suggested he be offered the uterine tea
jokes.punchline: Selina's new name for Catherine's tea: 'lesbian cooze potion'
jokes.explanation: The tea running gag continues with a new descriptor. 'Lesbian cooze potion' is crude but the escalating nomenclature for the tea is a fun running thread.

jokes.text: You know what I think? You sound like the world's gayest AM radio show.
jokes.setup: Selina and Gary are riffing about the tea, Gary agreeing with everything she says
jokes.punchline: Amy's outside perspective punctures the Gary/Selina symbiosis
jokes.explanation: Amy's observation is perfectly aimed — Gary and Selina's banter does resemble a cheerful morning radio dynamic. 'World's gayest AM radio show' is specific and quotable.

jokes.text: Well, he really likes Amy. — Amy who? — Ew! No!
jokes.setup: Strategizing about Leon West — 'he really likes Amy'
jokes.punchline: Amy's immediate horrified denial ('Amy who? Ew! No!') is funnier than any confirmation could be
jokes.explanation: Amy's sequence of denials is perfectly timed. 'Amy who?' is her first instinct (pretend it's a different Amy), followed by 'Ew! No!' when forced to confront it. Three words carry a lot of character.

jokes.text: Come on, nobody's asking you to engage in some sort of human pleasurable activity. — I know that's not your bag.
jokes.setup: Suggesting Amy flirt with Leon West
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual aside that human pleasurable activities aren't Amy's 'bag' — i.e., Amy is so wound up she's essentially asexual
jokes.explanation: A sharp Amy character burn. The phrasing 'human pleasurable activity' is deliberately clinical and distancing — it's as if Selina is talking about a different species. Character-integrated and economical.

jokes.text: One of these days, she's gonna make that face and that eyeball of hers is gonna pop out and shoot across the room. — Yeah. Boing!
jokes.setup: Selina has just made one of her signature intense/angry faces
jokes.punchline: Gary's prediction that the eye (which had surgery) will eventually shoot out; the 'Boing!' sound effect
jokes.explanation: The eye job callback lands as a visual gag. 'Boing!' is a perfect character moment from Gary — he's so comfortable around Selina that he can do sound effects about her face potentially malfunctioning.

jokes.text: Woodward minus Bernstein plus Propecia.
jokes.setup: Leon West has entered the room
jokes.punchline: Selina's mathematical insult formula: famous journalist (minus his partner) plus baldness remedy = a hack
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest insults. The mathematical formula structure ('X minus Y plus Z') is original for an insult, all three components are precisely chosen, and the whole thing takes 5 words. 'Woodward minus Bernstein' implies Leon is half of a great journalistic team — and not the better half. 'Plus Propecia' is the appearance kicker. Highly quotable and expertly crafted.

jokes.text: ...and I do 'cause I slept with him for two years... — Still off the record.
jokes.setup: Selina claims to remember her law school tort professor's lessons
jokes.punchline: The reason she remembers is that she slept with him; then immediately adds 'still off the record' as if this minor digression is the biggest threat
jokes.explanation: A quick character reveal that lands well. The 'still off the record' is the real joke — Selina applies the same secrecy demand to a throwaway admission as she does to state secrets. Very Selina.

jokes.text: You can go ahead. We're the 'Washington Post' and we have been sued by better terrible presidents than you. — Name one.
jokes.setup: Selina threatens to sue the Post
jokes.punchline: Leon's dismissal that better terrible presidents have tried; Selina's genuine challenge to name one
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed two-beat joke. 'Better terrible presidents' is an oxymoron that does real character work on Selina. Then 'Name one' is perfect — she's not disputing 'terrible,' she's disputing 'better,' which inadvertently concedes the criticism. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: You can have Amy. — Wait, I don't understand. — You can have her. — [long pause] — Probably better from behind, though, you know...
jokes.setup: Selina offers Amy to Leon as the price for spiking the story
jokes.punchline: Leon's confused pause, followed by Selina's unnecessarily graphic amendment — 'probably better from behind' — which she delivers to an increasingly horrified room
jokes.explanation: An outstanding cringe sequence. Selina casually offering Amy as a sexual commodity is horrible enough; her addition of 'probably better from behind' after a beat of silence turns it from shocking to genuinely hilarious. The structure (offer → confusion → clarification → worse) is perfectly executed. Leon's 'I don't understand' before the amendment makes the amendment funnier. High-end Veep.

jokes.text: Good evening. — Yeah, I don't blame you.
jokes.setup: After Selina's 'better from behind' comment, Leon simply leaves
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Yeah, I don't blame you' — she concedes the reasonableness of his exit while also suggesting it was about Amy's looks, not her own behavior
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the Leon scene. Selina's self-awareness is exactly calibrated — she's not remorseful, she's just acknowledging the situation pragmatically.

jokes.text: Can you stop blowing on my legs like that? — I'm drying the 'glow-tion.' — Don't use the word 'glow-tion.'
jokes.setup: Gary is attending to Selina's appearance preparation
jokes.punchline: Gary has invented the word 'glow-tion'; Selina forbids it
jokes.explanation: A small Gary scene-setter. His portmanteau 'glow-tion' (glow + lotion) is the kind of eager Gary invention that perfectly captures his servile enthusiasm. Selina's flat veto is the correct response.

jokes.text: Oh, it is beyond a dump. It is a toxic infant blowout out both diaper legs and up the back of the onesie.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if there's a second diary dump from Leon
jokes.punchline: Amy's extended infant diaper metaphor for the magnitude of the second leak
jokes.explanation: Amy's 'toxic infant blowout out both diaper legs and up the back of the onesie' is a memorably specific and disgusting metaphor. The specificity — 'both diaper legs AND up the back' — is what elevates it from generic to excellent. Very quotable.

jokes.text: It's got the soldier whose leg you lost. — Oh, my God, the lost leg. Ooh! — Surrounded by Mike's poorly drawn doodles of legs. — I'm sorry, Mike. — It looks like arms. — I'm better at arms. You should see the page where I wrote about the arms dealer.
jokes.setup: The diary revelations include a soldier whose leg Selina 'lost'
jokes.punchline: Mike's poorly drawn leg doodles are literally indistinguishable from arms; he pivots to noting he draws arms better — which is contextually catastrophic given the arms dealer entry
jokes.explanation: Exceptional multi-layered comedy. The arms/legs confusion becomes a pun when Mike notes he's better at arms — and 'arms dealer' takes the joke to a geopolitical level. Three escalating beats, all perfectly structured, culminating in a brilliant accidental double meaning. One of the episode's best sequences.

jokes.text: Why do you work for me, you stupid mustache?!
jokes.setup: Mike has just explained he wrote everything down to keep the lies straight
jokes.punchline: Selina's insult addresses Mike's facial hair as if it's a separate incompetent entity
jokes.explanation: Selina addresses Mike's mustache rather than Mike — a novel insult construction that implies the mustache itself is to blame. The specificity of 'stupid mustache' makes it memorable.

jokes.text: We were telling so many lies, I had to write them down to keep them straight. — Any decent lawyer would've told me to do that.
jokes.setup: Mike defending why he kept such a damaging diary
jokes.punchline: His completely backwards legal reasoning — a real lawyer would tell you the OPPOSITE
jokes.explanation: Mike's logic here is stunningly inverted — the whole point of a lawyer is to tell you NOT to write down your crimes. His confidence in citing 'any decent lawyer' while being catastrophically wrong is perfect Mike characterization.

jokes.text: They know that you 100% knew about the mining of those dead kids' social security and medical data. — No. — Well, I can't do the show. — I don't have a zinger for that one.
jokes.setup: A series of increasingly serious revelations; Mike has been offering zingers for each
jokes.punchline: Mike has no zinger prepared for the dead children data mining — the limit of his spin
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and one of Veep's finest Mike moments. 'I don't have a zinger for that one' is the most damning possible response to the revelation of mining dead children's data — it implies he HAS zingers for everything else. The joke works as dark political satire (there is no spin for child data exploitation) and as a perfect character beat. Highly quotable and structurally perfect.

jokes.text: Okay, look, Tiffany, I am so happy to be here, but unfortunately, I've just gotten this emergency call... — Pregnant daughter. — ...from my pregnant daughter... — The baby is in distress. — ...and her baby is in such distress.
jokes.setup: Selina needs to escape from a TV show booking; staffers prompt her on the excuse in real time
jokes.punchline: Selina is fed her lines in real-time while delivering them, creating a hybrid excuse-making/improvisation scene
jokes.explanation: A well-executed comedy scene — Selina being fed her lines and repeating them into the excuse is very funny. The irony that Catherine is actually having a medical emergency later makes this a structural payoff.

jokes.text: I was a patsy, but to my knowledge, this is just the tip of the iceberg. These revelations will continue to come out in a steady drip, drip, drip... — Balls. — ...like the leaky prison faucet I was forced to drink from.
jokes.setup: Mike is giving a dramatic statement to camera about the diary
jokes.punchline: Someone interjects 'Balls' mid-metaphor; Mike incorporates 'prison faucet' for added self-pity
jokes.explanation: Mike's self-dramatizing is undercut by the 'Balls' interjection. The 'prison faucet I was forced to drink from' is a nice detail — Mike sees himself as uniquely victimized and communicates this through increasingly overwrought metaphors.

jokes.text: Finally got Catherine down. — I could never get her down. She was always so scared about monsters everywhere, you know, under her bed and behind the curtain and in the closet. — Although, actually, one time, Andrew did hide one of his women in the closet, so she had reason to be somewhat alarmed.
jokes.setup: Selina recalls Catherine's childhood fear of monsters in the closet
jokes.punchline: Andrew (Selina's ex) literally did hide one of his mistresses in the closet — the monster fear was justified
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured misdirection. The setup is classic child-fears-monsters; the punchline reveals the fear was completely rational because Andrew hid his mistresses in the closet. Selina's deadpan 'so she had reason to be somewhat alarmed' is the perfect understatement. Deep character work on both Selina and Andrew.

jokes.text: Well, it's been quite a year for the Meyer Fund for Adult Literacy, AIDS, the Advancement of Global Democracy, Military Family Assistance, and Childhood Obesity.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about the foundation's work
jokes.punchline: Jaffar rattles off the full foundation name which includes 'AIDS' buried mid-list as if it's equivalent to literacy programs
jokes.explanation: The foundation name with AIDS randomly included is a background gag about how poorly organized the foundation is. Mild laughs — the joke is subtle to the point of being easy to miss.

jokes.text: Childhood obesity. You sent me an email about it last month at 4:00 AM. / I forgot. I was looking at Catherine's baby pictures.
jokes.setup: Jaffar recalls Selina's 4 AM email about childhood obesity
jokes.punchline: Selina's explanation: she was looking at Catherine's baby pictures — implying the photos of her own daughter as a child prompted a childhood obesity email
jokes.explanation: Devastatingly character-specific. Selina looking at her daughter's baby photos and being moved to 4 AM emails about childhood obesity — because presumably Catherine was a fat baby — is perfectly dark, perfectly Selina, and economically delivered.

jokes.text: From the moment I have left office, it has been nothing but a giant slalom down Mount McRimjob, brown diamond.
jokes.setup: Selina is venting to Jaffar about her post-presidency
jokes.punchline: The skiing metaphor with 'Mount McRimjob, brown diamond' — 'brown diamond' being a ski difficulty rating that also suggests a specific sexual act
jokes.explanation: A spectacular wordplay construction. 'Mount McRimjob' is already good; 'brown diamond' adds a double meaning (ski difficulty level / sexual act) that rewards the attentive viewer. The commitment to the skiing metaphor makes it funnier — Selina went all the way with it. Highly quotable and genuinely original.

jokes.text: You know, my uncle, he poisoned an entire village with sarin gas. — Ooh, that is worse. Thank you very much for that.
jokes.setup: Jaffar is trying to comfort Selina by saying she has nothing to be ashamed of
jokes.punchline: His comparator is his uncle poisoning a village with sarin gas; Selina genuinely appreciates the context
jokes.explanation: A perfect dark comedy moment. The casual mention of sarin gas village poisoning as a comforting comparator is a brilliant character note for Jaffar, and Selina's sincere 'Ooh, that is worse. Thank you very much for that' is the ideal reaction. Both characters are perfectly drawn in two lines.

jokes.text: I've got a plane that's fully fueled always and ready to go at any moment. — Why? Why is your plane always fueled? — Well, you know, in case... Let's just say that I've got some cousins... — Yeah. — ...who sometimes get very angry at some of your tall buildings.
jokes.setup: Jaffar suggests they leave the country on his always-fueled plane
jokes.punchline: The reason his plane is always fueled is implied to be terrorism (cousins who get angry at 'your tall buildings') — a 9/11 reference played deadpan
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most daring jokes. The slow build — always fueled → cousins → tall buildings — earns the punchline. The 'some of your tall buildings' euphemism is the masterstroke. Dark, specific, and perfectly structured. Veep at its most transgressive.

jokes.text: Dipshit Mike and his shit-dip diary. 'Amy committed perjury today,' written in crayon and mustard stains.
jokes.setup: Dan is discussing the diary leak at the bar
jokes.punchline: The inversion 'dipshit'/'shit-dip' plus the image of crayon and mustard stains as Mike's writing implements
jokes.explanation: Dan's 'dipshit/shit-dip' chiasmus is clever wordplay, and 'crayon and mustard stains' as the diary medium is a wonderfully specific image of Mike's incompetence. Character-specific to both Dan (mean, precise) and Mike (slovenly).

jokes.text: I wouldn't mind a little time in prison, get away from my wife, spend time with guys who get it. — Yeah, learn a trade.
jokes.setup: Mike is considering pleading guilty for his diary role
jokes.punchline: His reasons for prison being appealing — getting away from wife, camaraderie — undercut by 'learn a trade' as a practical bonus
jokes.explanation: Mike's prison fantasy is funny because it's both dark and oddly wholesome. 'Guys who get it' implies prison camaraderie; 'learn a trade' is the perfect practical afterthought.

jokes.text: What's next for the great Dan Egan? — Any job leads? — I thought about teaching high school, but, I mean, girls these days just can't keep a secret.
jokes.setup: Dan is asked about his future
jokes.punchline: He considered teaching high school but dismissed it because 'girls these days just can't keep a secret' — implying he'd pursue relationships with students
jokes.explanation: A perfectly dark Dan character moment. The joke is that Dan's objection to teaching is predatory logistics — he'd pursue female students but worries about discretion. Very Dan, very dark, very Veep. Economy of implication is excellent.

jokes.text: Yeah, she's telling Page Six that she and Brie are clam slamming. — Vaginas are so gross. — I wish I didn't have one. — Sometimes I forget I do. — Yeah, yeah, we all do.
jokes.setup: Dan reports gossip about Jane and Brie
jokes.punchline: Amy's escalating self-alienation from her own body ('I wish I didn't have one' → 'sometimes I forget I do') is met with Dan's casual 'yeah, we all do'
jokes.explanation: Amy forgetting she has a vagina is a brilliant character moment — she's so removed from her own femininity and humanity that this is credible. Dan's breezy 'yeah, we all do' is the perfect closer. The escalation (gross → wish I didn't → sometimes forget → we all do) is tight and character-perfect.

jokes.text: I gotta get up early, 'cause Selina's... — Yeah, make it a double. — Atta girl.
jokes.setup: Amy starts to explain why she has to get up early
jokes.punchline: She cuts herself off, orders a double instead — the mere mention of Selina is enough to necessitate more alcohol
jokes.explanation: A quiet but effective character beat. Amy doesn't even need to finish the sentence — the implication of Selina-related work is sufficient cause for a double. Dan's 'Atta girl' is fond and knowing.

jokes.text: Breaking news from the 'Washington Post'... Comes the revelation that it was President Selina Meyer and not President Laura Montez who negotiated with the Chinese president to free Tibet.
jokes.setup: Selina's world is collapsing from the diary; she's about to flee the country
jokes.punchline: The same diary that's been destroying her contains the redemptive revelation about Tibet — a perfect dramatic reversal
jokes.explanation: More structural comedy than a pure joke — the same source that's been destroying Selina all episode is now saving her. The Veep writers are using the diary as a comedic/dramatic device with both valences. The irony is very satisfying.

jokes.text: Well, the history books are being rewritten, and this time, it's not Texas saying Satan made fossils.
jokes.setup: Selina is overjoyed at the Tibet revelation
jokes.punchline: Her aside about Texas and creationism as the usual source of historical rewriting
jokes.explanation: A quick political aside that works as observational comedy. The Texas creationism dig is familiar territory but the 'Satan made fossils' phrasing is specific enough to get credit.

jokes.text: Yes, he did.
jokes.setup: Selina says history books are being rewritten, 'not Texas saying Satan made fossils'
jokes.punchline: Jaffar's sincere 'Yes, he did' — he believes Satan made fossils
jokes.explanation: Three words, ten out of ten economy. Jaffar's genuine belief that Satan made fossils is delivered with complete certainty and no irony. This is a perfect callback/topper — the setup dismisses creationism and the punchline character reveals a Mideast prince also believes it. Beautifully timed.

jokes.text: This is the best day we've had since I started working for you. — I guess it was actually good I lost the diary.
jokes.setup: The Tibet news has reversed Selina's fortunes
jokes.punchline: Richard's superlative enthusiasm; Mike's casual revisionism about the diary being a good thing
jokes.explanation: Mike's instant revisionism is perfectly shameless. 'I guess it was actually good I lost the diary' — he moves on instantly. Richard's sincere enthusiasm for Selina is also a reliable character beat.

jokes.text: We're getting requests for confirmation from all the big Tibetan papers, except for the 'Lhasa Express,' though. — They're playing their usual games.
jokes.setup: All media outlets want confirmation of the Tibet story
jokes.punchline: The 'Lhasa Express' is apparently a known problematic outlet that always 'plays games' — suggesting Richard has extensive familiarity with the Tibetan media landscape
jokes.explanation: A perfect Richard absurdist joke. The specificity of the 'Lhasa Express' and Selina's dismissive 'whatever' vs. Richard's insider knowledge ('their usual games') implies he has developed a deep familiarity with Tibetan media dynamics for no apparent reason. Very Richard.

jokes.text: Take that cum shot in all four eyes, Leon.
jokes.setup: Leon West is being embarrassed on live TV by the Tibet revelation
jokes.punchline: Selina's extremely vulgar celebration directed at Leon's TV image
jokes.explanation: 'All four eyes' implies Leon's glasses give him four targets — a specific and creative addition to an already crude celebration. Very Selina in its mix of vulgarity and precision.

jokes.text: You should've taken Amy and walked away. — Hmm? — Nothing.
jokes.setup: Leon West on TV being humiliated
jokes.punchline: Selina's aside to herself — she genuinely offered Amy as barter and still thinks Leon made a mistake not taking the deal
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback. Selina's genuine belief that Leon made a bad business decision by not accepting Amy is both a character note and a structural payoff to the earlier scene.

jokes.text: If she were a man, you would call that confidence. — Yeah, tell it, sister! Kick him in the cunt. — I adore Jane McCabe.
jokes.setup: Jane McCabe on TV is defending Selina against Leon
jokes.punchline: Selina's enthusiastic support includes 'kick him in the cunt' — using feminist language and extreme violence simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Selina's feminist solidarity expressed through extreme gendered violence is a characteristic Veep joke — she's simultaneously progressive and nihilistic. 'Kick him in the cunt' is jarring and funny precisely because it follows the feminist sentiment.

jokes.text: Montez is not gonna be happy. — It's gonna put a real turd in her chalupa. — Oh, we should get Mexican for lunch.
jokes.setup: Observing that Montez won't like the Tibet revelation
jokes.punchline: The 'turd in her chalupa' idiom (riff on 'fly in the ointment'), followed by someone taking it literally as a lunch suggestion
jokes.explanation: A two-beat joke. 'Turd in her chalupa' is a good ethnic-food variant on the cliché, and the literal lunch suggestion is the perfect button. Fast and effective.

jokes.text: Yale University president's office? — Oh. — Okay, and she knows what this is regarding, Yale? — Give me that. I'm talking. Hello, this is President Meyer.
jokes.setup: A call comes in from Yale; Selina is not expecting it
jokes.punchline: Selina's instant physical grab for the phone — she doesn't wait for the call to be explained
jokes.explanation: A quick character beat — Selina's reflexive power-grab for the Yale call is very funny. 'Give me that. I'm talking.' is characteristically impulsive.

jokes.text: Guess who is interested, all of a sudden, in the Selina Meyer Presidential Library. — Shut up! — Andrew. — What? — Your ex. — No. Yale! — I thought you went to Smith. — No, it's in the book that you wrote. — Right, right, right, right.
jokes.setup: Amy reveals Yale is interested in the library
jokes.punchline: Gary wrongly guesses 'Andrew' then 'your ex,' revealing he thinks of Yale as a person; Selina confirms 'Yale' and Gary has to be reminded she went to Yale, not Smith
jokes.explanation: Gary not knowing Selina went to Yale despite writing her book is a wonderful character failure. The escalating wrong guesses ('Andrew' → 'your ex' → 'No. Yale!') are well-structured.

jokes.text: Hey, Kent, free at last, free at last. — I've been fired three times in my life... from the US Postal Service Office of Investigations, by the Seattle Seahawks, and by Jonah Ryan. — I cried each time. This time, it was tears of joy.
jokes.setup: Richard encounters Dan/Kent after being fired from Jonah's office
jokes.punchline: Richard's three firings escalate to absurdity (Postal Service → Seahawks → Jonah Ryan); the tears detail is perfect
jokes.explanation: A wonderful Richard character moment. The specificity of the three firings (Postal Investigations → Seahawks → Jonah) implies an absurdly varied career history. 'I cried each time. This time, it was tears of joy' is the perfect button — Richard's emotional honesty makes everything funnier.

jokes.text: I might get my teeth re-whitened. — Maybe we could start a new business, take our shit show on the road. — Yeah, easier than explaining yards after the catch to Steve Largent.
jokes.setup: Kent and Ben are considering their next steps
jokes.punchline: Kent's non sequitur about Steve Largent (NFL receiver) and yards after catch — an obscure sports reference that implies his previous consulting work was in sports
jokes.explanation: A subtle Kent joke for sports fans. The Steve Largent reference is so specific it suggests Kent once tried to consult for football teams — a strange sidebar of his career history.

jokes.text: If we do this, though, one rule... no McLintock. — No shit.
jokes.setup: Ben proposes a new business with Kent
jokes.punchline: The one rule is 'no McLintock' — presumably a nightmare client — and Kent's instant 'no shit' suggests this is obvious
jokes.explanation: An inside joke for regular viewers — McLintock is apparently a known catastrophe. Works for the fanbase but loses impact without the reference context.

jokes.text: Maybe we could start a new business, take our shit show on the road. / Yeah, easier than explaining yards after the catch to Steve Largent.
jokes.setup: Dan proposes he and Kent start a new consulting venture
jokes.punchline: Kent's comparison: easier than explaining football statistics to former Seahawk Steve Largent — which implies he actually did this, connecting back to his Seahawks firing
jokes.explanation: A lovely callback that deepens the Seahawks firing joke. Steve Largent (a real former Seahawk turned congressman) is the perfect specific reference — Kent apparently spent time trying to explain analytics to him. The callback makes the earlier joke retroactively funnier.

jokes.text: The three Meyersketeers hang a shingle?
jokes.setup: Ben proposes the three of them start a business
jokes.punchline: 'Meyersketeers' — Three Musketeers with Meyer — is a portmanteau descriptor for their Selina-adjacent identities
jokes.explanation: A serviceable portmanteau. 'Meyersketeers' works and is easy to understand, but portmanteau jokes are common in Veep and this one isn't the sharpest.

jokes.text: I'm gonna bid you farewell because this face belongs in broadcasting and I start a new gig tomorrow. — Drinks are on you. Bye. — You'll be sorry.
jokes.setup: Dan announces he's leaving their bar conversation
jokes.punchline: His narcissistic 'this face belongs in broadcasting' followed by sticking them with the bill and 'You'll be sorry' — threats that reveal insecurity
jokes.explanation: Dan's exit is very Dan — grandiose self-assessment, financial maneuver, and a parting threat that hints at vulnerability. 'You'll be sorry' is a funny button on the whole exchange.

jokes.text: Am I Jewish? — Your circumcision was a success, Mr. Ryan.
jokes.setup: Jonah wakes up post-surgery confused
jokes.punchline: His first post-anesthesia question is existential ('Am I Jewish?'); the doctor answers with the medical success
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical joke. The confusion of religious identity with medical procedure is a good Jonah character beat — he's so uncertain about conversion that he needs to check if it's happened yet.

jokes.text: No erection for six weeks. — Well, I don't know how I'm not gonna get it hard when I'm talking about my hog with some hot 'shiska' nurse. — I'm your doctor, and it's shiksa. — I'll check on you later. — I'm pretty sure it's 'shiska.'
jokes.setup: Doctor gives post-circumcision instructions
jokes.punchline: Jonah's mispronunciation of 'shiksa' AND his use of it to describe his doctor while implying arousal — followed by insisting on the wrong pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Jonah's conversion is proceeding exactly as you'd expect — mispronouncing Yiddish terms, being inappropriately sexual with his doctor, and insisting he's right when corrected. The callback to the 'yamaka-ache' pattern of conversion mispronunciations adds value.

jokes.text: Well, I don't know how I'm not gonna get it hard when I'm talking about my hog with some hot 'shiska' nurse. / I'm your doctor, and it's shiksa. / I'll check on you later. / I'm pretty sure it's 'shiska.'
jokes.setup: The doctor warns Jonah about no erections for six weeks
jokes.punchline: Jonah's defense is discussing his genitals with nurses; he mispronounces 'shiksa'; the doctor corrects him and leaves; Jonah insists on his wrong pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Solid Jonah cringe comedy. The 'shiska/shiksa' mispronunciation is a callback to the 'yamaka-ache' setup; the doctor's tired correction and exit is a good reaction beat; Jonah's doubling down on the wrong pronunciation is perfect character stubbornness.

jokes.text: Babe, so far, being Jewish really sucks. — Will you pass me one of those ice packs, please? — Here you go, sweetie. — Thank you. — At least your dick won't smell like donkey pussy anymore. — Oh, God, I told you that was expired lube.
jokes.setup: Jonah post-circumcision is suffering
jokes.punchline: Shawnee's 'comfort' — that at least his dick won't smell like donkey pussy — and Jonah's explanation (expired lube)
jokes.explanation: The silver lining of circumcision being 'at least your dick won't smell like donkey pussy' is an original and deeply specific comfort. 'I told you that was expired lube' is the perfect response — the escalation from 'being Jewish sucks' to donkey pussy to expired lube is characteristically Jonah.

jokes.text: Wishes belong in the bottom of a well with unwanted girl children.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks if Uncle Jeff came to wish him a speedy recovery
jokes.punchline: Jeff's dismissal of wishes, via honor killing reference
jokes.explanation: Jeff's opening line is a show-stopper. 'Wishes belong in the bottom of a well with unwanted girl children' — the casual 'well' wish-making cliché inverted to reference honor killings — is extremely dark and perfectly constructed. Very quotable. Jeff is established as irredeemably evil in one sentence.

jokes.text: For shitting the urinal so badly, you made the Hindenburg look like a normal, on-time blimp landing.
jokes.setup: Jeff says he came to thank Jonah
jokes.punchline: His 'thank you' is for making such a catastrophic mess that it exceeded even the Hindenburg disaster — framed as a compliment
jokes.explanation: A genuinely brilliant insult. 'You made the Hindenburg look like a normal, on-time blimp landing' is perfectly calibrated — it recontextualizes an iconic disaster as a success, relative to Jonah. The delivery as a thank-you rather than an insult makes it funnier. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Uh, you can't talk to him like that. — Who is this tranny knuckle-dragger? — Somebody you hired to make sure you don't get erections? — That tranny knuckle-dragger is my fiancée.
jokes.setup: Shawnee defends Jonah from Jeff's insults
jokes.punchline: Jeff's immediate escalation to 'tranny knuckle-dragger' and 'erection prevention' — which Jonah then throws back by proudly introducing her as his fiancée
jokes.explanation: The exchange works as character comedy but the slur used is a weak shortcut. Jonah's defensive 'fiancée' announcement is the best part. Earned_vs_cheap score is lower because the trans slur is a cheap shock tactic.

jokes.text: Shut the fuck up, you epileptic Picasso painting!
jokes.setup: Jonah starts to defend himself about the shutdown
jokes.punchline: Jeff's art insult — Picasso painting (distorted) + epileptic (uncontrolled) = Jonah's physical presence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest insults. 'Epileptic Picasso painting' perfectly captures Jonah's gangly, disproportionate physique by combining art (distorted faces/bodies) with neurological disorder (uncontrolled movement). Highly original insult construction, extremely quotable.

jokes.text: That tranny knuckle-dragger is my fiancée. / Jonah! / Her name is Shawnee Tanz, and you will treat her with respect. / Tanz as in Sherman Tanz? / That's right. He's my father. / Oh, royalty.
jokes.setup: Jonah defends Shawnee from Jeff's insult
jokes.punchline: Jeff's instant pivot to 'royalty' upon learning her father's name — replacing all contempt with sycophantic recognition of money
jokes.explanation: Good structural joke. The speed of Jeff's total personality reversal on hearing 'Sherman Tanz' is the punchline — going from 'tranny knuckle-dragger' to 'royalty' in one beat. Pure power-worship comedy.

jokes.text: I suppose I should say I'm sorry. — Thank you. — Sorry you're even related to that human melted candle who puts the Jew in 'why people hate Jews.'
jokes.setup: Jeff appears to be apologizing to Shawnee
jokes.punchline: The apology is actually for her being related to Sherman Tanz (her own father), who he then demolishes with 'human melted candle who puts the Jew in why people hate Jews'
jokes.explanation: A masterfully structured fake apology. The setup implies remorse; the punchline pivots entirely to attack Sherman Tanz. 'Human melted candle' is an inspired physical description, and 'puts the Jew in why people hate Jews' is a devastating anti-Semitic self-reference — Jeff using Jewish self-criticism as an insult against another Jew. Exceptional misdirection and construction.

jokes.text: I am pulling this creature from the jizz lagoon right off the New Hampshire congressional ballot and replacing him with his cousin, Ezra.
jokes.setup: Jeff announces he's replacing Jonah on the ballot
jokes.punchline: 'Creature from the jizz lagoon' — riff on Creature from the Black Lagoon — as Jonah's descriptor
jokes.explanation: A good horror-movie parody insult. 'Jizz lagoon' as a substitute for 'Black Lagoon' implies Jonah's origins are ignoble. The specificity of replacing him with 'his cousin, Ezra' adds a local political texture.

jokes.text: Ezra has more raw political talent in the tip of his rosy-head pecker than you have in this mangled abortion coat hanger you should be ashamed to call your body.
jokes.setup: Jeff is selling Ezra as the superior candidate
jokes.punchline: The 'compliment' to Ezra is framed as a single penis tip vs. Jonah's entire body (described as a 'mangled abortion coat hanger')
jokes.explanation: A spectacular compound insult. The comparison structure (tip of Ezra's penis > all of Jonah) is elegant, and 'mangled abortion coat hanger you should be ashamed to call your body' is a vividly horrible physical description. Jeff's insults are consistently the episode's most baroque.

jokes.text: Ezra has more raw political talent in the tip of his rosy-head pecker than you have in this mangled abortion coat hanger you should be ashamed to call your body.
jokes.setup: Uncle Jeff explains why Ezra is replacing Jonah
jokes.punchline: The insult accelerates from a penis-tip comparison to 'mangled abortion coat hanger' as a description of Jonah's entire body — layers of anatomical, procedural, and object references piled into one sentence
jokes.explanation: 'Mangled abortion coat hanger you should be ashamed to call your body' is an extraordinary insult — it combines (1) physical description (mangled, elongated), (2) a disturbing procedural reference (abortion coat hanger = back-alley abortion tool), and (3) the shame element. The body-as-object construction is inventive and deeply specific to Jonah's tall, ungainly frame. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: The people of New Hampshire are gonna be so grateful to me, I'm gonna be like a disability check wrapped around a pack of no-filter cigarettes.
jokes.setup: Jeff is explaining how popular his intervention will be
jokes.punchline: His understanding of New Hampshire voters is a disability check and no-filter cigarettes — a class-coded, devastating portrait
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. The simile is completely original — a disability check wrapped around cigarettes is a perfect encapsulation of a certain class of voters, delivered with total confidence by a robber baron who views them as marks. It's simultaneously a political joke, a class joke, and a character moment. Extremely quotable and genuinely inventive.

jokes.text: Okay, yeah, that makes sense. My teachers had to do that all the time. — No, with us... You and me. — What?
jokes.setup: Shawnee says she wants to 'slow things down'
jokes.punchline: Jonah interprets 'slow things down' as a teaching-pace issue (his teachers slowed down for him), not a romantic slowdown
jokes.explanation: An elegantly constructed character beat. Jonah's misunderstanding reveals so much — that he's been slowed down for repeatedly in school, that he can't conceive of being dumped, and that his self-awareness is genuinely near zero. The setup and subversion are clean.

jokes.text: Is that before or after I scheduled the surgery to cut my dick off so you could marry me?
jokes.setup: Shawnee says she's had doubts for 'a little while'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's framing of the circumcision as 'cutting his dick off' — demonstrating his utter misunderstanding of what happened
jokes.explanation: Jonah describing his circumcision as 'cutting my dick off' is a perfect character beat — his hyperbolic self-pity and medical misunderstanding are both Jonah-specific. The timing (during the breakup) escalates it perfectly.

jokes.text: What the fuck does that even mean? Is that before or after I scheduled the surgery to cut my dick off so you could marry me?
jokes.setup: Shawnee says she's had doubts 'for a little while'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's devastated response that he scheduled the surgery (circumcision = 'cutting his dick off' in his mind) in service of marriage, making the breakup timing exquisitely painful
jokes.explanation: Jonah describing his circumcision as 'scheduling surgery to cut his dick off' captures his perspective on the sacrifice with perfect Jonah-level hyperbole. The timing irony — having just had surgery to commit to her — is well-constructed pathos-comedy.

jokes.text: I'm so glad I came in person and didn't call. — Goodbye, Jonah. — Sorry. — Come on, honey, I'll walk you out. — Oh, that's nice of you. — Are you parked in the garage?
jokes.setup: Shawnee has just broken up with Jonah in front of Uncle Jeff
jokes.punchline: Jeff escorts Shawnee out and makes small talk about parking while Jonah sits alone in the hospital
jokes.explanation: A masterfully cruel scene. Jeff walking Shawnee out and chatting about parking while Jonah is left post-breakup, post-circumcision, alone in a hospital bed is perfect dark comedy. 'I'm so glad I came in person and didn't call' as Jeff's button is the best possible exit. Jeff prefers the cruelty of in-person delivery.

jokes.text: I'm so glad I came in person and didn't call. — Goodbye, Jonah. — Sorry. — Come on, honey, I'll walk you out. — Oh, that's nice of you. — Are you parked in the garage?
jokes.setup: Jonah has just been dumped; Shawnee prepares to leave
jokes.punchline: Uncle Jeff's 'I'm so glad I came in person and didn't call' — savoring the in-person viewing of Jonah's devastation — and then immediately pivoting to walking Shawnee out with faux-chivalry ('Are you parked in the garage?')
jokes.explanation: Uncle Jeff's line 'I'm so glad I came in person' is darkly brilliant — he attended explicitly to enjoy Jonah's suffering and is now openly luxuriating in it. His pivot to helping Shawnee ('Are you parked in the garage?') treats the nephew's romantic devastation as a footnote. Character integration is exceptional. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Ow! Nurse! [Jonah alone in hospital room]
jokes.setup: Jonah is alone in his hospital room after being dumped and threatened with ballot removal
jokes.punchline: His cry of pain and call for a nurse — having apparently hurt himself — is the final indignity capping his complete humiliation
jokes.explanation: Visual/physical capper on the Jonah storyline. Presumably he's gotten an erection despite the doctor's orders — which would be both painful post-circumcision and ironic given the dumping. The joke is implied and functional.

jokes.text: Leon West just called for the seventh time. He says he wants you to... — He wants to gargle my nuts and tickle my taint? — Tell him to get in line.
jokes.setup: Leon keeps calling; staffer starts to relay the message
jokes.punchline: Selina interrupts with a profane and specific prediction; 'Tell him to get in line' implies others want the same thing
jokes.explanation: Selina's triumphant post-Tibet confidence expressed through extreme profanity is characteristically Veep. 'Get in line' as the button is effective.

jokes.text: Oh, ma'am, the publisher said we're getting some book reviews in. — Oh, the 'Lhasa Express' gives it five namastes.
jokes.setup: Reviews are coming in for Selina's book
jokes.punchline: Richard has already received a review from the 'Lhasa Express' — the same outlet that was 'playing games' earlier — and their system is 'namastes'
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback to the 'Lhasa Express' running gag. The fact that they've already reviewed it, and use 'namastes' as their rating scale (five namastes!), is the kind of absurdist world-building that Veep does brilliantly with Richard as the vehicle.

jokes.text: Ma'am, Catherine's in an ambulance. — She's having some bad bleeding. — Oh, my God, on the couch? — Did they put the garbage bags down?
jokes.setup: Catherine is having a medical emergency
jokes.punchline: Selina's first concern is the couch/garbage bags — a callback to the French linen joke — even in a genuine crisis
jokes.explanation: A devastating callback. Selina's daughter is in an ambulance and her first question is about the couch. The garbage bag callback lands with full force because we remember the setup. Perfect character integration — only Selina would react this way.

jokes.text: One of us should go? — Well, what are you standing here for? — You're the donor. — Oh, father.
jokes.setup: Catherine is in an ambulance; someone should go to the hospital
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately redirects to Jaffar as the 'donor' (sperm donor) — she's been thinking of him in those terms only
jokes.explanation: Selina calling Jaffar 'the donor' instead of a parent or husband figure reveals how she conceptualizes the relationship. Jaffar's 'Oh, father' — processing his role designation — is a good button.

jokes.text: Do you need to leave? — Where do I stand? — Oh, right there on the mark. — On the thing? Okay. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Jaffar has been told he should go to the hospital but is now being directed to a TV mark
jokes.punchline: Jaffar's literal obedience to 'where do I stand?' — taking it as a staging question rather than an existential one — while Catherine is in an ambulance
jokes.explanation: A good comedic beat of cross-purposes. 'Where do I stand?' as both existential and literal question is a nice double meaning. Jaffar's cheerful compliance with TV blocking while his partner is in an ambulance is quietly absurd.

jokes.text: Hi, there. Welcome to 'CBS This Ride.' I'm your host, Dan Egan, bringing you exclusive content... right here in New York City Yellow Cabs. — Oh, no, no, no, no. Off. — ...Sir, how do you... I can't get the screen to go off. — Come on, how the fuck do you turn this thing off?!
jokes.setup: Dan said earlier 'this face belongs in broadcasting'; he now appears on a cab TV screen
jokes.punchline: His new gig is a cab TV show — 'CBS This Ride' — confirming his downfall while being uncancellable in the literal sense
jokes.explanation: A perfect structural joke and one of the episode's best. Dan said 'this face belongs in broadcasting' and his new gig is a cab TV screen. 'CBS This Ride' echoes 'CBS This Morning' perfectly. The escalation of his career collapse, the irony of him being on a screen that can't be turned off, and the passenger's escalating fury all work together. Outstanding.

jokes.text: Yes, in sleeping with my trainer, I did not exercise good judgment.
jokes.setup: Mike's mediocre zinger was introduced earlier; Selina said she wouldn't use it
jokes.punchline: She uses it on The Tonight Show anyway — and the studio audience loves it
jokes.explanation: The callback is better than the joke itself. Selina using Mike's pun after explicitly rejecting it is a good character moment — she'll use what works. The studio audience reaction within the show validates the pun she had dismissed.

jokes.text: By the way, this is quite a photo on the cover. — It's really, really nice. — Thank you. — Do I need, like, special glasses to be able to see this in focus? — Or how does this work?
jokes.setup: The Tonight Show host admires the book cover
jokes.punchline: He implies the cover photo is so airbrushed/manipulated it requires special glasses to see in focus
jokes.explanation: A polite passive-aggressive dig at Selina's appearance/photo manipulation. Gets the job done but isn't especially inventive — it's a standard 'too airbrushed' joke.

jokes.text: 'Publisher Weekly' calls it 'Scattered and disjointed, much like the Meyer presidency.'
jokes.setup: A sweet eight-year-old girl reads from a cue card
jokes.punchline: She reads a pointed political criticism of Selina's presidency to her face
jokes.explanation: The second child review is better than the first — the 'scattered and disjointed like the presidency' is both a book review AND a political judgment, delivered by a child who has no idea what she's saying.

jokes.text: The 'New York Post' says, 'Remainder City.'
jokes.setup: Ruby is the third child reviewer
jokes.punchline: 'Remainder City' — two words predicting the book will be heavily discounted — is the most devastating review and the most economical
jokes.explanation: Two words. 'Remainder City.' This is among the episode's very best jokes — the New York Post's whole review is a two-word prediction of commercial failure. 'Remainder City' as a publishing term delivered by a ten-year-old girl is perfectly calibrated. The economy is a 10 — no word is wasted.

jokes.text: 'The New Yorker' called it 'A trite, monotonous tune that's as inconsequential as any...' — I am still a young woman. Yeah. — Looks at my hands. — Gorgeous. — Look at my neck. — Okay.
jokes.setup: The fourth child reads an even worse New Yorker review
jokes.punchline: Selina physically demonstrates her youth mid-review — hands and neck — while the host just says 'Okay'
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of Selina displaying her hands and neck as proof of youth during a devastating review is a perfect cringe escalation. The host's defeated 'Okay' is the ideal response. The visual comedy is inferred but unmistakable — she's literally holding up body parts as evidence.

jokes.text: The library staircase goes right up and smashes through the glass ceiling. — Exactly what it's like to be a woman.
jokes.setup: Selina is describing the architectural metaphor for her presidential library
jokes.punchline: 'Smashes through the glass ceiling' — the feminist metaphor becomes literal and also subtly violent (smashes)
jokes.explanation: A decent feminist joke. The literalization of 'smashing the glass ceiling' as an architectural feature is a nice touch, though it's a familiar enough cliché that the inversion doesn't earn maximum points.

jokes.text: How come you never talk about your stay in Arizona? — Leon West will not let go. — He follows me around and thinks about me 24/7, nonstop. — Cuckoo.
jokes.setup: The host asks a pointed question from Leon West
jokes.punchline: Selina's response reframes Leon's investigative journalism as obsession, then dismisses him with 'Cuckoo'
jokes.explanation: A competent Selina spin moment. Reframing accountability journalism as stalking is very on-brand, and 'Cuckoo' as the button is delivered with perfect TV-friendly condescension.

tmdb_overview: Selina's book finally arrives. Jonah has a big meeting. Dan, Ben and Kent grab a drink.
insight: Escalation-driven character chaos sustains 78.8 despite lacking memorable standout punchlines.
